title: Circadian disruption in rotating shifts
content: Rotating shift schedules disrupt circadian alignment and increase fatigue-related errors, especially during rapid day-night transitions.

title: Protected sleep after nights
content: After night duty, protecting a primary daytime sleep block is strongly associated with better alertness and reduced cumulative fatigue.

title: Strategic naps
content: Naps of 20 to 30 minutes can improve alertness and reduce performance deficits in shift workers without major sleep inertia.

title: Caffeine half-life timing
content: Caffeine can impair sleep when consumed too close to intended sleep onset; a practical cutoff window is around six hours.

title: Light as circadian signal
content: Bright light after wake and reduced light before sleep are high-impact tools for circadian phase support.

title: Short turnaround risks
content: Less than 11 hours between shifts is associated with poorer recovery, higher fatigue, and increased safety risk.

title: Drowsy driving after night shifts
content: Commutes after overnight work carry meaningful drowsy-driving risk; mitigation includes naps, caffeine timing, and buddy checks.

title: Consecutive nights and sleep debt
content: Multiple consecutive night shifts commonly produce cumulative sleep debt that worsens mood, cognitive performance, and recovery.

title: Decompression and stress downshift
content: A short decompression routine after shift can lower hyperarousal and support faster sleep initiation.

title: Social support effects
content: Peer or buddy check-ins can reduce perceived stress and improve adherence to recovery actions during high-strain periods.
source: Mental health and shift work studies
